critic_raw:
This CNN vs. MLR experiment for hyperspectral crop yield prediction has a well-conceived spatial-temporal split rationale, but the implementation contains important flaws. The split strategy is described as 'spatio-temporal stratified split with stratified seasonal random sampling,' but then operationally specifies 'randomly sampling field-seasons while maintaining proportions.' This is effectively a stratified random split, not a temporal split — field-seasons from the same year can appear in both training and test sets. This does not respect temporal ordering and allows future yield data to influence model training through shared spatial correlates.

Second, the preprocessing description contains a contradiction. The design states 'PCA transformation is fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets' — correct practice. However, earlier in the same section it describes using 'agronomically-informed band selection (e.g., NDVI, GNDVI)' as an alternative, without specifying whether this selection is data-driven or fixed a priori. If band selection is data-driven (e.g., correlation-based), it must be fit on training data only, not on the full dataset.

Third, the CNN operates on 'hyperspectral image patches' but yield is measured at the field level. The aggregation strategy from patch-level predictions to field-level yield estimates is not specified. Without a clear aggregation protocol, patch-level predictions may not aggregate correctly to the field-season yield unit, creating a unit mismatch between model output and evaluation target.

Finally, the baseline (MLR with Ridge/Lasso) uses the same dimensionality-reduced features as the CNN but the CNN operates on raw image patches (2D spatial structure). This means the CNN has access to spatial context that the MLR baseline cannot use — the comparison does not isolate feature representation from spatial modeling capacity.
all_issues_raised: Split strategy described as spatio-temporal but operationally implemented as stratified random sampling — field-seasons from the same year can appear in both train and test, violating temporal ordering
Ambiguity in band selection: if data-driven band selection (correlation with yield) is used instead of fixed agronomic indices, it must be fit on training data only — this is not clearly specified
Unit mismatch: CNN operates on image patches but yield is field-level; no aggregation protocol is specified for patch-to-field prediction

Feature asymmetry: CNN receives 2D spatial structure from image patches while MLR receives flattened dimensionality-reduced features — spatial modeling capacity is conflated with representation learning advantage
Secondary baseline (persistence model) is evaluated but no statistical significance test is specified for comparing CNN vs. MLR RMSE — bootstrap CIs alone are insufficient

Spatial data leakage risk: random assignment of field-season pairs without spatial separation allows adjacent fields with correlated soil/climate to appear in both training and test sets, inflating apparent generalization
Ambiguity in when agronomic band selection occurs relative to splitting — if selection uses full-dataset correlations, this constitutes feature selection leakage before model training

Bootstrap confidence intervals computed over spatially autocorrelated field-season pairs will be anti-conservative (too narrow); block-bootstrapping by spatial cluster is required for valid uncertainty estimates
Persistence baseline description is ambiguous about whether validation-season data is used to compute its predictions, potentially introducing leakage into the baseline evaluation
No multi-seed or multi-split stability analysis reported for the CNN; a single random split with spatial correlation may produce a favorable or unfavorable test partition

Spatial leakage risk: random allocation of field-season pairs does not guarantee spatial separation between train and test fields; spatially proximate fields can share soil and environmental characteristics, inflating test performance
Patch-to-field aggregation method for CNN predictions not specified — different aggregation strategies (mean, max, learned) materially affect performance and must be defined to ensure reproducibility
Field ID as embedding/fixed effect may absorb field-level variance and create information leakage if the same fields appear in both train and test sets across different seasons

CNN captures spatial texture in addition to spectral information while MLR uses only spectral features — comparison conflates spatial modeling capacity with spectral feature quality, making attribution of advantage unclear

Yield label standardization and inverse transformation could interact with RMSE bootstrap in non-trivial ways if inverse transform introduces asymmetric errors; this should be reported in original units throughout
all_issues_adjudicated: Split strategy described as spatio-temporal but operationally implemented as stratified random sampling — field-seasons from the same year can appear in both train and test, violating temporal ordering

CNN captures spatial texture in addition to spectral information while MLR uses only spectral features — comparison conflates spatial modeling capacity with spectral feature quality, making attribution of advantage unclear
